`libgit2` functions return large negative numbers, representing non-libgit2 errors (usually network errors), but the current Enum type (`LibGit2.Error.Code`) cannot contain them.

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/5835c3b69e4e0f47eeb9a512d91622b50ad3423c/stdlib/LibGit2/src/error.jl#L37
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/5835c3b69e4e0f47eeb9a512d91622b50ad3423c/stdlib/LibGit2/src/error.jl#L82-L86
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/5835c3b69e4e0f47eeb9a512d91622b50ad3423c/stdlib/LibGit2/src/error.jl#L103-L107

An `ArgumentError: invalid value for Enum Code` is thrown when the error code `abs(err_code) > 40`.
An example: https://buildkite.com/julialang/julia-master/builds/42812#01939979-4dae-44ed-9e39-1f693a963576/863-969
```

```

Perhaps the type restriction on `GitError.code` could be relaxed to cover these errors.

```patch
 struct GitError <: Exception 
     class::Class 
-    code::Code 
+    code::Union{Code, Int32}
     msg::String 
 end 
```

> When appended to a variable on the left-hand side of an assignment, or as part of a local declaration, the :: operator means something a bit different: it declares the variable to always have the specified type

But this does not seem to be true for abstract types:
```jl
b::Real = 1
b = 1.0
b = '1'
b = "1"
```

`b` does not have type Real, instead it can have any concrete subtype of Real.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56809/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56809/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2538228942","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56809#issuecomment-2538228942","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56809","id":2538228942,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6XSkzO","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-12T08:49:05Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T08:49:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"My understanding of the documentation is that `b::T = x` ensures that `b isa T` will always hold.

In your example:
```julia
julia> b::Real = 1
1

julia> b = 1.0
1.0

julia> b isa Real
true

julia> b = '1'
'1': ASCII/Unicode U+0031 (category Nd: Number, decimal digit)

julia> b isa Real # even though  '1' isa Real == false
true
```

IIRC, any assignment to a variable declared as `b::T` translates to
```julia
b = convert(T, x)::T
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2538228942/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520","id":2647538643,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6dzjvT","number":56520,"title":"Julia on servers without IPv4","user":{"login":"tk3369","id":1159782,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNTk3ODI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1159782?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369","html_url":"https://github.com/tk3369","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tk3369/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-10T19:11:04Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T19:43:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I am trying to build julia 1.11.1 on a server that only has IPv6 interfaces. I was able to build the julia binary but the networking related tests were skipped. And, they were skipped because `getipaddr()` throws an exception:

```julia
julia> getipaddr()
ERROR: No networking interface available
```

This is where it's invoked from the test:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/8f5b7ca12ad48c6d740e058312fc8cf2bbe67848/test/choosetests.jl#L188-L202

I do have IPv6 addresses:
```
julia> getipaddrs()
3-element Vector{IPAddr}:
...
```

The documentation suggest that it should return an IP address, just having a preference of IPv4 over IPv6. 
```julia
help?> getipaddr
search: getipaddr getipaddrs getpid getindex getalladdrinfo

  getipaddr() -> IPAddr

  Get an IP address of the local machine, preferring IPv4 over IPv6. Throws if no addresses are available.

  getipaddr(addr_type::Type{T}) where T<:IPAddr -> T

  Get an IP address of the local machine of the specified type. Throws if no addresses of the specified type are available.

  This function is a backwards-compatibility wrapper around getipaddrs. New applications should use getipaddrs instead.

  Examples
  ≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡

  julia> getipaddr()
  ip"192.168.1.28"
  
  julia> getipaddr(IPv6)
  ip"fe80::9731:35af:e1c5:6e49"

  See also getipaddrs.
```

So, why doesn't getipaddr() return IPv6 address instead? Do you consider this a bug or an error in documentation? I feel that it's the former.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2466872779","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520#issuecomment-2466872779","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56520","id":2466872779,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6TCX3L","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-10T19:43:30Z","updated_at":"2024-11-10T19:43:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Both `getipaddr()` and `getipaddr(::Type{<:IPAddr})` are confusing to me.

`getipaddr(T)` should return an address of type `T`. We already request a list of addresses of that type on line 284:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/afdba951b36e65cd8b728e460160d8765aae321f/stdlib/Sockets/src/addrinfo.jl#L283-L293
So if, `T` is IPv4, all addresses in `addrs` should be IPv4 addresses, and then we don't need to `findfirst` (on line 291) address of type IPv4 – the first one will be an IPv4 address. On the other hand, if `T` is IPv6, then none of `addrs` will be IPv4 and (even if some would) the method should return an IPv6 address because that was requested.

The single-argument method should not "prefer" one address type over another, because the address type is given by the argument. 

The method without any argument should prefer IPv4 as per documentation and it does not do that now. It either returns IPv4 or nothing. 

We should change the methods to something like
```julia
function getipaddr(addr_type::Type{T}) where T<:IPAddr
    addrs = getipaddrs(addr_type)
    if length(addrs) == 0
        error("No networking interface available")
    end
    return first(addrs)
end
function getipaddr()
    ipv4addrs = getipaddrs(IPv4)
    isempty(ipv4addrs) || return first(ipv4addrs)
    return getipaddr(IPv6)
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2466872779/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Before this change, `getipaddr()` would throw an error unless there is an `IPv4` address assigned to a network interface. After this change, `getipaddr()` preferably returns an `IPv4` address, but if none exists, it returns an `IPv6` address.

Additionally, the PR simplifies the definition of `getippadr(T)`.

PR updates the definition of `getipaddr()` so that the documentation is respected.

Before this change, `getipaddr()` would throw an error unless there is an `IPv4` address assigned to a network interface. After this change, `getipaddr()` preferably returns an `IPv4` address, but if none exists, it returns an `IPv6` address.

Additionally, the PR simplifies the definition of `getippadr(T)`.

bug","user":{"login":"pda","id":15759,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NzU5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/15759?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda","html_url":"https://github.com/pda","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pda/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This pull request fixes a missing-comma-separator bug in the [Bootleg JSON writer](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/7eb5cb89fb938a1dc67efa3861b25562767a7bbe/test/buildkitetestjson.jl#L13) 🏴‍☠️ which is responsible for producing JSON test results for [Buildkite Test Engine](https://buildkite.com/platform/test-engine/).

There was a bug when serializing to JSON array (from `AbstractVector`) where elements that were [`===` / “egal”](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#Core.:===) to the final element would not have a comma separator placed after them, producing invalid JSON. I stumbled upon this because I work at Buildkite; I've previously [contributed a fix for a different issue in `test/buildkitetestjson.jl](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53706).

Specifically, this bug occurred when the last item of `expanded` or `backtrace` within `failure_expanded` was an empty string, because `elt === last(val)` was `"" === ""` was `true`, so e.g. `["hello", "", "world", ""]` would serialize to:

```json
[
  "hello",
  ""
  "world",
  ""
]
```

Note this was _not_ happening when the last element was a **non**-empty string; the strings come from `split(string, '\n')` which returns a singleton empty string for zero-width splits, but separate strings for non-empty splits:

```
julia> [[x, objectid(x)] for x in split("\n", '\n')]
2-element Vector{Vector{Any}}:
 ["", 0x37373e5ed3d3af9c]
 ["", 0x37373e5ed3d3af9c]

julia> [[x, objectid(x)] for x in split("hello\nhello", '\n')]
2-element Vector{Vector{Any}}:
 ["hello", 0x8096d78ab9fd726c]
 ["hello", 0x2bec5d6f24b3bdf5]
```

This patch changes the “is final item” check to be index-based, rather than identity-based.

I've tested it by adding a synthetic failure to an existing test:

```julia
@test throw(ErrorException("testing Buildkite JSON\n\ncomma after blank lines\n\nwhen last line is blank\n"))
```

And then observed the `test/results_1.json` before and after this patch:

```diff
       "expanded": [
         "testing Buildkite JSON",
-        ""
+        "",
         "comma after blank lines",
-        ""
+        "",
         "when last line is blank",
         ""
       ]
```

Related:
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53706
- cc: @tecosaur for past context

Note this was _not_ happening when the last element was a **non**-empty string; the strings come from `split(string, '\n')` which returns a singleton empty string for zero-width splits, but separate strings for non-empty splits:

```
julia> [[x, objectid(x)] for x in split("\n", '\n')]
2-element Vector{Vector{Any}}:
 ["", 0x37373e5ed3d3af9c]
 ["", 0x37373e5ed3d3af9c]

julia> [[x, objectid(x)] for x in split("hello\nhello", '\n')]
2-element Vector{Vector{Any}}:
 ["hello", 0x8096d78ab9fd726c]
 ["hello", 0x2bec5d6f24b3bdf5]
```

This patch changes the “is final item” check to be index-based, rather than identity-based.

I've tested it by adding a synthetic failure to an existing test:

```julia
@test throw(ErrorException("testing Buildkite JSON\n\ncomma after blank lines\n\nwhen last line is blank\n"))
```

And then observed the `test/results_1.json` before and after this patch:

```diff
       "expanded": [
         "testing Buildkite JSON",
-        ""
+        "",
         "comma after blank lines",
-        ""
+        "",
         "when last line is blank",
         ""
       ]
```

Related:
- https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53706
- cc: @tecosaur for past context

I disagree. The current documentation exactly says that: "Return true if the value of the sign of x is negative, otherwise false.".

I agree with @mikmoore  that `signbit` should not be used for a generic implementation of `x > 0` (but it can be used for a specialized implementation of `x > 0` for some types). I think the distinction between `signbit` and `sign` is confusing because both of them deal with a *sign*, just on a different level (or in a different context).

Being a mathematical operation, `sign` is consistent with the comparisons against zero; `signbit` is not due to zero and special inputs. Nevertheless, `signbit` and `sign` are equivalent for non-zero and non-special inputs. We already have such a pair of functions: `isequal` and `==`. We could draw inspiration from the docs of `isequal`:
> Similar to ==, except for the treatment of floating point numbers and of missing values. isequal treats all floating-point NaN values as equal to each other, treats -0.0 as unequal to 0.0, and missing as equal to missing. Always returns a Bool value.

The definition of `signbit` could be:
> Return `true` if the sign of the representation of `x` is negative, otherwise `false`.
> 
> This function is similar to `sign`, except for the treatment of floating point numbers. For example, `signbit` treats both `-NaN` or `-0.0` as negative and both `NaN` or `0.0` as positive.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2485699023/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

assignment without any error","user":{"login":"ArmanZarrin97","id":120459964,"node_id":"U_kgDOBy4SvA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/120459964?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97","html_url":"https://github.com/ArmanZarrin97","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArmanZarrin97/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":338330521,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMzgzMzA1MjE=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/needs%20more%20info","name":"needs more info","color":"e0d5f0","default":false,"description":"Clarification or a reproducible example is required"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-10-14T14:58:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-15T09:47:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi,

I tried to run this line in one of my functions:

` J[:, isl]=[ki... zeros(length(setdiff(1:nBus,ipq)))...]`

J is a sparse matrix with size `120*120`. J[:, isl] is a column with only four values, which are [11.00 -11.00 44.00 -44.00]. [ki... zeros(length(setdiff(1:nBus,ipq)))...] is a matrix `120*1`, of which 23 elements are 50.00 and the others equal to zero. The result of running this code is again a column with only four values, which are [0.00 0.00 0.00 0.00]. However, I expect it to be a column with 23, 24,..., or 27 values, which includes 23 values equal to 50.00. 

I didn't encounter any error. I believe it should be a bug. By chance I understood that this assignment doesn't work correctly, But it could be in fact dangerous for the others. 

I fixed it in this way:

`J[:, isl]=[ki... zeros(length(setdiff(1:nBus,ipq)))...]**[:,1]**`

Best,
Arman

```julia
using SparseArrays
A = zeros(3, 1)
sparseA = sparse(A)
b = ones(1, 3)
A[:, 1] = b
sparseA[:, 1] = b

# A is:
# 3×1 Matrix{Float64}:
#  1.0
#  1.0
#  1.0

# sparseA is:
# 3×1 SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int64} with 1 stored entry:
#  1.0
#   ⋅   
#   ⋅   
```
Notice that `b` is a 1×10 matrix. ```julia
"""
    isdivisible(m, n)

Return true if `m` is divisible by `n`.
"""
isdivisible(m::Integer, n::Integer)
```

It can be implemented as `iszero(rem(m, n))` but that's not the optimal implementation for all types from Base (it allocates for a `BigInt`). 

AbstractAlgebra.jl from Nemo implements this exact function: https://github.com/Nemocas/AbstractAlgebra.jl/blob/396fdddbc1d1620a7b2866e7340e39b14228176e/src/julia/Integer.jl#L121-L151
I would like to have it in Base.

(Discussed on Discourse: https://discourse.julialang.org/t/checking-divisibility-efficiently/121418)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56212/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56212/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

does not provide a function for checking divisibility:
```julia
"""
    isdivisible(m, n)

Return true if `m` is divisible by `n`.
"""
isdivisible(m::Integer, n::Integer)
```

It can be implemented as `iszero(rem(m, n))` but that's not the optimal implementation for all types from Base (it allocates for a `BigInt`). 

AbstractAlgebra.jl from Nemo implements this exact function: https://github.com/Nemocas/AbstractAlgebra.jl/blob/396fdddbc1d1620a7b2866e7340e39b14228176e/src/julia/Integer.jl#L121-L151
I would like to have it in Base.

There are some divisibility checks in Base.

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d36417b8230f7f56359f8607495ec97247bcab50/base/array.jl#L2455
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d36417b8230f7f56359f8607495ec97247bcab50/base/arrayshow.jl#L225
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d36417b8230f7f56359f8607495ec97247bcab50/base/int.jl#L117
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d36417b8230f7f56359f8607495ec97247bcab50/base/int.jl#L137
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d36417b8230f7f56359f8607495ec97247bcab50/base/range.jl#L1209
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/d36417b8230f7f56359f8607495ec97247bcab50/base/reinterpretarray.jl#L444

Some are in stdlibs (e.g., checking leap year in Dates).

I wouldn't say this is exactly "meaningfully simplified" (after all, how much can you simplify a divisibility check?):
```diff
- if sizeof(T) % sizeof(S) == 0
+ if isdivisible(sizeof(T), sizeof(S))
```
It is more explicit, maybe. I would say it is similar to replacing `x % 2 == 0` with `iseven(x)`.

I am mostly interested in it for the `BigInt` type for which `x % n` allocates, and so `x % n == 0` is not cheap, whereas it is cheap for `Int`s.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2420290519/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I never realized `length` does this. I guess I always assumed it returned the number of elements along the first dimension (influenced by python's `len` perhaps).

What's the motivation for `prod(size(A))` then? [People use it.](https://juliahub.com/ui/Search?q=prod%5C(size%5C(.%2B%5C)%5C)&type=code&rx=true) 

Is it true that any `prod(size(A))` can be replaced by `length(A)`?

I believe the pattern `prod(size(A)[c:d]` cannot be replaced by `length`, but `size(A)[c:d]` should never return `()`, unless `c>d`, which seems like a very marginal case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2330694565/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

My apologies, I'll hide my previous comments. Let me make it clear for any other confused contributors.

The issue is not returning the multiplicative identity for an empty collection. That is the standard definition in math. And programming languages (R, Common Lisp, numpy) follow that definition, including Julia:
```julia
julia> prod(Int[])
1
```
The documentation for `prod` says that:
> The value returned for empty itr can be specified by init. It must be the multiplicative identity (i.e. one) as it is unspecified whether init is used for non-empty collections.



The issue is *guessing what the multiplicative identity is for an empty tuple*. We do not guess it for `Any[]`, but we guess it for `Tuple(Any[])`.
```julia
julia> A = []
Any[]

julia> prod(A)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching one(::Type{Any})

julia> prod(Tuple(A))
1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2330922475/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

     for out-of-bounds indices. The user is responsible for checking it manually.
     Only use `@inbounds` when you are certain that all accesses are in bounds (as
     undefined behavior, e.g. crashes, might occur if this assertion is violated). For
     example, using `1:length(A)` instead of `eachindex(A)` in a function like
     the one above is _not_ safely inbounds because the first index of `A` may not
     be `1` for all user defined types that subtype `AbstractArray`.
+
+!!! note
+    `@inbounds` eliminates bounds checks in methods called within a given block, but not
+    those that are syntactically within the given block.
+    However, keep in mind that the `@inbounds` context propagates only one function call
+    layer deep. For example, if an `@inbounds` block includes a call to `f()`, which in turn
+    calls `g()`, bounds checks that are syntactically within `f()` will be eliminated,
+    while ones within `g()` will not.

This commit changes the current `parse(::AbstractString)` to `parse(::String)` and implements `parse(::AbstractString)` by converting the argument to `String`.

Now, even `LazyString`s can be parsed to Markdown representation.

Fixes {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55810","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55810/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55810/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55810/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55810","id":2534926513,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6XF-ix","number":55810,"title":"`@which` and dispatch disagree in obscure case","user":{"login":"Socob","id":1915511,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5MTU1MTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1915511?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob","html_url":"https://github.com/Socob","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Socob/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-09-18T23:09:39Z","updated_at":"2024-09-19T07:50:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Preface: I apologize if this is report is not useful. It’s not something that would be encountered during “normal”/supported use, but since it might point to a latent problem in the dispatch logic, I thought it’d be good to point it out.

So, I’ve found this strange case:
```julia
julia> struct S{N}
           x::Float64
           y::NTuple{N, Float64}
       end

julia> func(T) = "Any"
       func(T::Type{<: Tuple}) = "Tuple"
func (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> for field_type in fieldtypes(S)
           T = field_type isa UnionAll ? field_type.body : field_type
           println("which:  ", @which func(T))
           println("actual: ", func(T))
       end
which:  func(T) @ Main REPL[2]:1
actual: Any
which:  func(T::Type{<:Tuple}) @ Main REPL[2]:2
actual: Any
```
In other words, for the second `struct` field, `@which` says that `func(::Type{<: Tuple})` should be called, whereas what’s actually called is `func(::Any)`. Bizarrely, this does not happen if the `Tuple` type is the *first* `struct` field:
```julia
julia> struct S{N}
           y::NTuple{N, Float64}
           x::Float64
       end

julia> func(T) = "Any"
       func(T::Type{<: Tuple}) = "Tuple"
func (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> for field_type in fieldtypes(S)
           T = field_type isa UnionAll ? field_type.body : field_type
           println("which:  ", @which func(T))
           println("actual: ", func(T))
       end
which:  func(T::Type{<:Tuple}) @ Main REPL[2]:2
actual: Tuple
which:  func(T) @ Main REPL[2]:1
actual: Any
```

I realize that the `body` field is internal and the resulting type is invalid (?) in this case – see preface above.

{"action":"opened","number":55822,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55822","id":2083070710,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM58KSL2","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55822","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55822.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55822.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55822","number":55822,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Fix import-before-module check","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"PR #51635 introduced function `check_src_module_wrap` which checks whether a module declaration is missing or whether a `using`/`import` is declared before a module.

The original implementation, based on manual checks via regexes, did not catch all possible cases.  In particular, it did not consider multi-line comments, e.g.,
```
using Foo
=#
module Bar
end
```
threw errors even though it should be valid.

Single-line comments, multi-line comments, and docstrings make it quite difficult to reliably check that a module is declared and not preceded by any using/import.  Some examples are
```
module B end

=#
using A
=#
module B end

using A
=#
module B end

"docs" module B end
```

Because of that, this commit avoids all manually-written, specialized conditions and employs `Meta.parse` to parse the code into `Expr`s.  It then becomes very simple to check the condition of interest.  The downside is increased runtime.

[WITHOUT the shortcut; see bellow] I measured the time it takes to check three files of different sizes (the first measurements is the current version, the second is master):
```
 # 63979 lines of code
@btime Base.check_src_module_wrap(Base.PkgId("dummy"), "LibSCIP.jl")
  102.267 ms (535826 allocations: 36.19 MiB)
  5.630 μs (8 allocations: 528 bytes)

 # 1051 lines of code
@btime Base.check_src_module_wrap(Base.PkgId("dummy"), "Primes.jl")
  3.525 ms (21503 allocations: 1.08 MiB)
  5.946 μs (9 allocations: 656 bytes)

 # 79 lines of code
@btime Base.check_src_module_wrap(Base.PkgId("dummy"), "BenchmarkTools.jl")
  106.170 μs (578 allocations: 46.02 KiB)
  3.240 μs (8 allocations: 560 bytes)
```
The slowdown is *dramatic* (most of the time is spent reading the source file into a String).

I added a condition that checks if the file content starts with "module ", in which case the function returns immediatelly.  This shortcut helps: LibSCIP.jl takes only 5.865 μs (instead of 102.265 ms). Of course, the shortcut is taken only in some cases...

I am submitting this PR anyway for discussion (CC: original author @IanButterworth).

Fixes {"action":"closed","number":55822,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55822","id":2083070710,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM58KSL2","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55822","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55822.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55822.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55822","number":55822,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Fix import-before-module check","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"PR #51635 introduced function `check_src_module_wrap` which checks whether a module declaration is missing or whether a `using`/`import` is declared before a module.

The original implementation, based on manual checks via regexes, did not catch all possible cases.  In particular, it did not consider multi-line comments, e.g.,
```
#=
using Foo
=#
module Bar
end
```
threw errors even though it should be valid.

Single-line comments, multi-line comments, and docstrings make it quite difficult to reliably check that a module is declared and not preceded by any using/import.  Some examples are
```
#= =# using A
module B end

#= #=
=#
using A
=#
module B end

"docs" module B end
```

Because of that, this commit avoids all manually-written, specialized conditions and employs `Meta.parse` to parse the code into `Expr`s.  It then becomes very simple to check the condition of interest.  The downside is increased runtime.

[WITHOUT the shortcut; see bellow] I measured the time it takes to check three files of different sizes (the first measurements is the current version, the second is master):
```
 # 63979 lines of code
@btime Base.check_src_module_wrap(Base.PkgId("dummy"), "LibSCIP.jl")
  102.267 ms (535826 allocations: 36.19 MiB)
  5.630 μs (8 allocations: 528 bytes)

 # 1051 lines of code
@btime Base.check_src_module_wrap(Base.PkgId("dummy"), "Primes.jl")
  3.525 ms (21503 allocations: 1.08 MiB)
  5.946 μs (9 allocations: 656 bytes)

 # 79 lines of code
@btime Base.check_src_module_wrap(Base.PkgId("dummy"), "BenchmarkTools.jl")
  106.170 μs (578 allocations: 46.02 KiB)
  3.240 μs (8 allocations: 560 bytes)
```
The slowdown is *dramatic* (most of the time is spent reading the source file into a String).

I added a condition that checks if the file content starts with "module ", in which case the function returns immediatelly.  This shortcut helps: LibSCIP.jl takes only 5.865 μs (instead of 102.265 ms). Of course, the shortcut is taken only in some cases...

I am submitting this PR anyway for discussion (CC: original author @IanButterworth).

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55885","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55885/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55885/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55885/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55885","id":2549931784,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6X_N8I","number":55885,"title":"Line continuation character inside `@lazy_str` does not continue lines","user":{"login":"thchr","id":26799210,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2Nzk5MjEw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26799210?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr","html_url":"https://github.com/thchr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thchr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":269951426,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyNjk5NTE0MjY=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:strings","name":"domain:strings","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":""Strings!""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-09-26T08:46:28Z","updated_at":"2024-09-26T12:49:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The `@lazy_str` macro behaves differently from ordinary strings when using the line continuation character `\`:

```jl
lstr = lazy"hello \
       world"       # == "hello \\\n       world"
str  = "hello \
       world"       # == "hello world"
```

Apart from interpreting the line-continuation character as a "raw" backslash (and consequently also inserting a line-break), `lazy"..."` also doesn't ignore the white-space on the following line. This seems unintended and undesirable. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379","id":2448100963,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6R6w5j","number":55379,"title":"False array-argument lcm for non-integers","user":{"login":"cyanescent","id":161313975,"node_id":"U_kgDOCZ10tw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/161313975?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent","html_url":"https://github.com/cyanescent","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/cyanescent/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-08-05T09:42:22Z","updated_at":"2024-08-05T13:00:56Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The extension of lcm() and gcd() to both Array and Rational was incorrect. The case of the gcd has been corrected in #34417, but not the lcm:

```julia
lcm([1//2;1//2])
1//1 # false 

lcm(1//2,1//2)
1//2 # true
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2269022462","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379#issuecomment-2269022462","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55379","id":2269022462,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6HPoj-","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-05T13:00:55Z","updated_at":"2024-08-05T13:00:55Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The problem shows up even for a singleton array:
```julia
julia> lcm([1//2])
1//1
```
See the implementation:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/40ecf690197a7a6221cfcffd9c74799258fb4495/base/intfuncs.jl#L153
(The problem is with the `init` argument; in this case, `init` is `1//1` and `lcm(1//1, 1//2)` is `1//1`.)

Neither `lcm` nor `gcd` is documented to accept an array argument by the way. We only have 
>  gcd(x, y...)
>
>  Greatest common (positive) divisor (or zero if all arguments are zero). The arguments may be integer and rational numbers.

and

>  lcm(x, y...)
>
>  Least common (positive) multiple (or zero if any argument is zero). The arguments may be integer and rational numbers.

What should the methods return when the input array is empty? Currently,
```julia
julia> gcd(Int[])
0

julia> lcm(Int[])
1

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53614","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53614/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53614/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53614/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53614","id":2170279943,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5oxi2Z","number":53614,"title":"Allowing disabling the default prompt suffix in `Base.getpass()`","user":{"login":"JamesWrigley","id":5361518,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUzNjE1MTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5361518?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley","html_url":"https://github.com/JamesWrigley","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JamesWrigley/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-03-05T22:57:38Z","updated_at":"2024-05-07T14:46:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53614","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53614","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53614.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53614.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"By default `Base.getpass()` will append `: ` to the prompt, but sometimes that's undesirable, so now there's a `with_suffix` keyword argument to disable it.

For context, my use-case is showing SSH prompts received from a server verbatim to the user. I did attempt to write a test for this but it would've required a lot of refactoring since `getpass()` is pretty hardcoded to expect input from `stdin` :\ An alternative design would be to have a `suffix=": "` argument instead, but I couldn't think of a good usecase for a user putting the prompt suffix in a separate argument instead of `message` It is found that the contents of the if-lese branch must be exactly the same before the error will be reported to line 0.

```

    a = [0.5 1 3]
    d = -1
    c = maximum(a)
    if c < d
        c = maximum[a]
    else
        c = maximum[a]
    end
end

test_maximum()

```julia
julia> function test_maximum()
           a = [0.5 1 3]
           d = -1
           c = maximum(a)
           if c < d
               c = maximum[a]
           else
               c = maximum[a]
           end
       end
test_maximum (generic function with 1 method)

julia> test_maximum()
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching getindex(::typeof(maximum), ::Matrix{Float64})
Stacktrace:
 [1] test_maximum()
   @ Main ./REPL[1]:0
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.3
Commit 0b4590a5507 (2024-04-30 10:59 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 8 × Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-8550U CPU @ 1.80GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, skylake)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 8 virtual cores)
```

Also, as OP said, changing one branch leads to the correct line number being reported:
```julia
julia> function test_maximum()
           a = [0.5 1 3]
           d = -1
           c = maximum(a)
           if c < d
               c = 1. # changed here
           else
               c = maximum[a]
           end
       end
test_maximum (generic function with 1 method)

julia> test_maximum()
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching getindex(::typeof(maximum), ::Matrix{Float64})
Stacktrace:
 [1] test_maximum()
   @ Main ./REPL[6]:8
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[7]:1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2124039748/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022","id":2235768914,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6FQyBS","number":54022,"title":"BUG: `clamp(typemax(UInt64), Int64)` errors","user":{"login":"KlausC","id":6223854,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMjM4NTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6223854?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC","html_url":"https://github.com/KlausC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-04-10T14:38:50Z","updated_at":"2024-04-10T16:02:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The call `clamp(typemax(UInt64), Int64)` should return `typemax(Int64)` but throws `InexactError`.

```
julia> clamp(typemax(UInt64), Int64)
ERROR: InexactError: check_top_bit(UInt64, -9223372036854775808)
Stacktrace:
 [1] throw_inexacterror(f::Symbol, ::Type{UInt64}, val::Int64)
   @ Core ./boot.jl:634
 [2] check_top_bit
   @ ./boot.jl:648 [inlined]
 [3] toUInt64
   @ ./boot.jl:759 [inlined]
 [4] UInt64
   @ ./boot.jl:789 [inlined]
 [5] convert
   @ ./number.jl:7 [inlined]
 [6] clamp
   @ ./math.jl:98 [inlined]
 [7] clamp(x::UInt64, ::Type{Int64})
   @ Base.Math ./math.jl:123
 [8] top-level scope
   @ REPL[1]:1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2047933717","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022#issuecomment-2047933717","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54022","id":2047933717,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM56EP0V","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-10T16:02:26Z","updated_at":"2024-04-10T16:02:26Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"It's due to `ifelse` being used in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/e9a24d4cee4153023a7841a75fc4285229f26720/base/math.jl#L99-L102

Since `ifelse` is a function, all arguments are evaluated. That is
```julia
convert(UInt64, typemax(Int64))
convert(UInt64, typemin(Int64))
convert(UInt64, typemax(UInt64))
```
with the second one throwing the reported error since `typemin(Int64)` is negative. We might replace `ifelse` with the ternary conditional operator, but I am not sure whether it has some performance consequences.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2047933717/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

julia> min(typemin(Int), typemin(UInt))
ERROR: InexactError: convert(UInt64, -9223372036854775808)
Stacktrace:
 [1] throw_inexacterror(func::Symbol, to::Type, val::Int64)
   @ Core ./boot.jl:772
 [2] check_sign_bit
   @ ./boot.jl:778 [inlined]
 [3] toUInt64
   @ ./boot.jl:889 [inlined]
 [4] UInt64
   @ ./boot.jl:919 [inlined]
 [5] convert
   @ ./number.jl:7 [inlined]
 [6] _promote
   @ ./promotion.jl:375 [inlined]
 [7] promote
   @ ./promotion.jl:400 [inlined]
 [8] min(x::Int64, y::UInt64)
   @ Base ./promotion.jl:494
```
How should we resolve the case when the result is not representable in the promoted type?

I understand that `clamp(x::Int64, ::Type{UInt64}) = UInt64(max(x, 0))` would work, but is it the right solution?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2049235451/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

this case can be fixed just by reordering the comparisons:
```julia
function clamp(x::X, lo::L, hi::H) where {X,L,H}
    T = promote_type(X, L, H)
    v = (x < lo) ? convert(T, lo) : convert(T, x)
    return (v > hi) ? convert(T, hi) : v
end
```
Let me know if you can think of other edge-cases. I'll try to come up with some myself.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2049287347/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

potential issue is when `x`, `lo`, or `hi` cannot be represented in `T = promote_type(X, L, H)`: one of them is negative and `T` happens to be unsigned. That is possible only if at least one of `X`, `L`, `H` is an unsigned type with at least as many bits as the maximum number of bits of the other, signed types, e.g., `promote_type(UInt64, Int32, Int64)` (which is `UInt64`) or `promote_type(UInt128, Int64, Int64)` (which is  `UInt128)`, but not `promote_type(UInt32, Int64, Int64)` (which is `Int64`). 

With the `clamp` version from my previous post, there are three possible cases that result in an error:
| condition       | failed convert   | example call                             |
|-----------------|------------------|------------------------------------------|
| `x < lo`        | `convert(T, lo)` | `clamp(Int64(-2), Int64(-1), UInt64(1))` |
| `lo <= x <= hi` | `convert(T, x)`  | `clamp(Int64(-2), Int64(-3), UInt64(1))` |
| `x > hi `       | `convert(T, hi)` | `clamp(UInt64(1), Int64(-4), Int64(-3))` |

Note that if we permit `lo < hi`, there is another possible error if `0 > x > hi` and `lo` is unsigned, e.g., `clamp(Int64(-1), UInt64(1), Int64(-3))`.

All of the examples listed here also fail in Julia v1.10.2.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2049389432/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

the method `clamp(x, ::Type{T}) where T<:Integer` really necessary?

> The promotion to a common type have to be done by explicit conversions.

So you don't see the following methods failing as an issue?
```julia
clamp(Int64(-1), Int64(-2), UInt64(1))
clamp(Int64(-2), Int64(-1), UInt64(1))
clamp(UInt64(1), Int64(-4), Int64(-3))
```

Please can you add the test cases:

Yes, I will!

> julia> clamp(2.0^63, Int)
> ERROR: MethodError: no method matching rem(::Float64, ::Type{Int64})
> ```
> The last one indicates IMO a bug in `clamp(x,::Type{<:Integer}` but is avoided by the implementation I proposed.

That's due to `% T` in https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/1ae41a2c0a3ba49d4b39dc4933dddf952b5f7f3c/base/math.jl#L123

What if we simply changed that definition to 
```julia
clamp(x, ::Type{T}) where {T<:Integer} = convert(T, clamp(x, typemin(T), typemax(T)))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2051669724/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+# doi:10.1023/b:bitn.0000014561.58141.2c
+function givensAlgorithm(f::Number, g::Number)

Quick testing indicates a performance difference:
```julia

julia> f = big(2)
2

julia> g = big(3.0)
3.0

julia> @btime one(promote_type(typeof($f), typeof($g)))
  32.062 ns (2 allocations: 104 bytes)
1.0

julia> @btime one(first(promote($f, $g)))
  68.061 ns (4 allocations: 208 bytes)
1.0

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296","id":2267794293,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6HK8t1","number":54296,"title":"`Base.binomial` returns `Float64` for `Integer` inputs of different types.","user":{"login":"sgaure","id":18311483,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4MzExNDgz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/18311483?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure","html_url":"https://github.com/sgaure","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sgaure/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/maths","name":"maths","color":"93755D","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-04-28T20:13:36Z","updated_at":"2024-04-29T07:59:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The docs say 

```
binomial(n::Integer, k::Integer)

  The binomial coefficient \binom{n}{k}, being the coefficient of the kth term in the polynomial
  expansion of (1+x)^n.
```
which isn't strictly true. When `n` and `k` have different integer types, the generalized binomial coefficient is returned, i.e. from `binomial(x::Number, k::Integer)`.

```
julia> binomial(4, 2)
6

julia> binomial(Int32(4), 2)
6.0

julia> binomial(Int32(4), Int32(2))
6
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2082100389","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296#issuecomment-2082100389","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54296","id":2082100389,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM58GlSl","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-29T07:59:32Z","updated_at":"2024-04-29T07:59:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Add
```julia
binomial(n::Integer, k::Integer) = binomial(promote(n, k)...)
```
?
Then
```julia
julia> binomial(Int32(4), Int64(2)) 
6 # typeof(6) == Int64
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2082100389/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35067

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35067

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

It's faster for some complicated Real types.
```julia
julia> f(x) = x<=zero(typeof(x))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> g(x) = signbit(x)||iszero(x)
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  99.055 ns (4 allocations: 144 bytes)
true

julia> @btime g(x) setup=(x=rand(BigFloat))
  10.700 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
false
```

faster kronecker product between hermitian and symmetric matrices","user":{"login":"araujoms","id":997998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk5Nzk5OA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/997998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms","html_url":"https://github.com/araujoms","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/araujoms/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-02-05T12:19:21Z","updated_at":"2024-02-05T13:56:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53186","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53186","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53186.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53186.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"The kronecker product between complex hermitian matrices is again hermitian, so it can be computed much faster by only doing the upper (or lower) triangular. As @andreasnoack will surely notice, this only true for types where `conj(a*b) == conj(a)*conj(b)`, so I'm restricting the function to act only on real and complex numbers. In the symmetric case, however, no additional assumption is needed, so I'm letting it act on anything.

Benchmarking showed that the code is roughly 2 times as fast as the vanilla kronecker product, as expected. The fastest case was always the UU case, and the slowest the LU case. The code I used is below
```julia
using LinearAlgebra
using BenchmarkTools
using Quaternions

randrmatrix(d, uplo = :U) = Hermitian(randn(Float64, d, d), uplo)
randcmatrix(d, uplo = :U) = Hermitian(randn(ComplexF64, d, d), uplo)
randsmatrix(d, uplo = :U) = Symmetric(randn(ComplexF64, d, d), uplo)
randqmatrix(d, uplo = :U) = Symmetric(randn(QuaternionF64, d, d), uplo)

dima = 69
dimb = 71
for randmatrix in [randrmatrix, randcmatrix, randsmatrix, randqmatrix]
    for auplo in [:U, :L]
        for buplo in [:U, :L]
            a = randmatrix(dima, auplo)
            b = randmatrix(dimb, buplo)
            c = kron(a,b)
            therm = @belapsed kron!($c, $a, $b)
            C = Matrix(c)
            A = Matrix(a)
            B = Matrix(b)
            told = @belapsed kron!($C, $A, $B)
            @show told/therm
        end
    end
end
```
Weirdly enough, I got this expected speedup in one of my machines, but when running the benchmark in another I got roughly the same time. I guess that's a bug with `BechmarkTools`, because that's not consistent with the times I get running the functions individually, out of the loop.

Another issue is that although I added a couple of tests, I couldn't get them to run. Perhaps someone here can tell me what's going on? I could run the tests from LinearAlgebra, it's just that editing the files made no difference to what was being run. I did get hundreds of errors from `triangular.jl`, but that's untouched by my code.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53186/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53186/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1927068843","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53186#issuecomment-1927068843","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53186","id":1927068843,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5y3Lyr","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-05T13:56:52Z","updated_at":"2024-02-05T13:56:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I could run the tests from LinearAlgebra, it's just that editing the files made no difference to what was being run.

want a non-mutating version of `append!` that puts two sequences together.


```jl
julia> (append(x::T, y::T) where T<: AbstractVector) = append!(copy(x), y);

julia> (append(x::T, y::T) where T<:AbstractString)= join([x, y]);

julia> append([:a, :b], [:c, :d])
4-element Vector{Symbol}:
 :a
 :b
 :c
 :d

julia> append("ab", "cd")
"abcd"
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53045","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53045/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53045/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53045/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53045","id":2099225436,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM59H6Nc","number":53045,"title":"`front` and `tail` for `AbstractVector`","user":{"login":"jariji","id":96840304,"node_id":"U_kgDOBcWqcA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96840304?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji","html_url":"https://github.com/jariji","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-01-24T22:32:47Z","updated_at":"2024-01-25T08:30:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```jl
Base.front(x::AbstractVector) = x[begin:end-1]
Base.tail(x::AbstractVector) = x[begin+1:end]
```

Currently these are only defined for `Tuple` but it would be nice to have them for `AbstractVector` too.

 valtype(A::Type{<:AbstractArray}) = eltype(A)
 
+"""
+    prevind(::AbstractArray, i)
+
+Return the index before `i`, e.g. equivalent to `i - 1` for an integer `i`. This
+function can be useful for generic code that operates on both arrays and
+strings, since string indices may not be consecutive.
+
+!!! warning
+    The returned index is not guaranteed to be a valid index of the passed
+    `AbstractArray`. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53113","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53113/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53113/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53113/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53113","id":2107007072,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM59lmBg","number":53113,"title":"Parts of sorting broken due to `scratch` kwarg","user":{"login":"palday","id":1677783,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2Nzc3ODM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1677783?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday","html_url":"https://github.com/palday","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/palday/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":4342669890,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABkWpM8AAAABAtfiQg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/sort","name":"sort","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Put things in order"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-01-30T05:51:27Z","updated_at":"2024-01-30T12:42:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I noticed that sorting in AxisKeys.jl is broken. I'm posting here because it seems related to the changes in sorting that happened in Julia 1.9 and the relevant piece of the error (keyword argument `scratch`, see below) does not occur in AxisKeys.jl, but rather from `Base.Sort.__sort!`.

MWE:
```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(; temp=true)
@info "Installing packages"
Pkg.add(["AxisKeys"]; io=devnull)
using AxisKeys

data = rand(2, 10, 3)
A = KeyedArray(data; channel=[:left, :right], time=range(13, step=2.5, length=10), iter=31:33)
sort!(A; dims=1)
```

Error:
```julia
julia> sort!(A; dims=1)
ERROR: TypeError: in keyword argument scratch, expected Union{Nothing, Vector{<:Integer}}, got a value of type Vector{Float64}
Stacktrace:
 [1] sort!(A::KeyedArray{…}; kw::@Kwargs{…})
   @ AxisKeys ~/.julia/packages/AxisKeys/QsNqy/src/functions.jl:274
 [2] __sort!(A::KeyedArray{…}, ::Val{…}, alg::Base.Sort.MissingOptimization{…}, order::Base.Order.ForwardOrdering, scratch::Vector{…})
   @ Base.Sort ./sort.jl:1879
 [3] sort!(A::KeyedArray{…}; dims::Int64, alg::Base.Sort.MissingOptimization{…}, lt::Function, by::Function, rev::Nothing, order::Base.Order.ForwardOrdering, scratch::Vector{…})
   @ Base.Sort ./sort.jl:1866
 [4] top-level scope
   @ REPL[8]:1
Some type information was truncated. Use `show(err)` to see complete types.
```

Notably, the error does not happen if the eltype of the array is `Int`.

I bisected to this:

```
commit cee0a0494c70208b6cd5a32ccdf75d954a429870
Author: Lilith Orion Hafner <lilithhafner@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat Dec 3 06:19:20 2022 +0600

    Refactor and document sorting dispatch (#47383)
    
    * create an internal `_sort!` function and use it (rename the existing `_sort!` to `__sort!`)
    
    * test for several of bugs that slipped through test suite
    
    * Give each sorting pass and DEFAULT_STABLE a docstring
    
    * add pretty printing for the new algorithms that are much more flexible than the old ones
    
    * fix unexpected allocations in Radix Sort
    
    fixes #47474
    in this PR rather than separate to avoid dealing with the merge
    
    * support and test backwards compatibility with packages that depend in sorting internals
    
    * support 3-, 5-, and 6-argument sort! for backwards compatibility
    
    * overhall scratch space handling
    
    make _sort! return scratch space rather than sorted vector
    so that things like IEEEFloatOptimization can re-use the
    scratch space allocated on their first recursive call
    
    * test handling -0.0 in IEEEFloatOptimization
    
    * fix and test bug where countsort's correct overflow behavior triggers error due to unexpected promotion to UInt

 base/sort.jl    | 1287 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++--------------------
 test/sorting.jl |  171 +++++++-
 2 files changed, 968 insertions(+), 490 deletions(-)
```

It is initially created here in Base: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/75c38e3fdb0875bf0f5df3a7f0f6625556c9e2e8/base/sort.jl#L2096
as `Vector{Float64}(undef, size(A, 1))` (notice Float64). A bunch of sorting logic then follows until it gets to sorting a vector that is a view of the original array in a particular dimension. It hits [this line of AxisKeys](https://github.com/mcabbott/AxisKeys.jl/blob/cd0318440200b42e30b0d4f860bd8392318aec0f/src/functions.jl#L274):
```julia
perm = sortperm(parent(A); kw...)
```
which simply passes all keyword arguments it received from a function in Base (including `scratch` from above).

It ends up calling this method https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/75c38e3fdb0875bf0f5df3a7f0f6625556c9e2e8/base/sort.jl#L1868-L1875
(notice `Integer` in `scratch::Union{Vector{<:Integer}, Nothing}`), and fails.

I am not sure what to do about it, but hopefully this might help someone with debugging.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1916764730/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

  15.739 ms (3 allocations: 68.66 MiB) # PR
```

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1378960147","pull_request_review_id":1708476809,"id":1378960147,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5SMUMT","diff_hunk":"@@ -567,4 +568,25 @@ function which(program_name::String)
 end
 which(program_name::AbstractString) = which(String(program_name))
 
+"""
+    Sys.username() -> String
+
+Return the username for the current user. If the username cannot be determined
+or is empty, this function throws an error.
+
+To retrieve a username that is overridable via an environment variable,
+e.g., `USER`, consider using
+```julia
+user = get(Sys.username, ENV, "USER")
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.11"
+    This function requires at least Julia 1.11.
+"""
+function username()
+    pw = Libc.getpw()
+    isempty(pw.username) && throw(ErrorException("empty username"))","path":"base/sysinfo.jl","commit_id":"06e8108ea145c70800569a0a6ba39739679f3ba6","original_commit_id":"06e8108ea145c70800569a0a6ba39739679f3ba6","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

behavior"},{"id":163368715,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjMzNjg3MTU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/parser","name":"parser","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":"Language
