syntax"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-11-03T13:09:41Z","updated_at":"2023-11-03T13:34:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
julia> if true println("hello")
           1
       end
hello
1

julia> Meta.@lower if true println("hello")
           1
       end
:($(Expr(:thunk, CodeInfo(
    @ none within `top-level scope`
1 ─     goto #3 if not true
    @ REPL[13]:1 within `top-level scope`
2 ─     println("hello")
│   @ REPL[13]:2 within `top-level scope`
└──     return 1
3 ─     return nothing
))))
```
The `println("hello")` expression becomes part of the body of the true branch.  This "feature" is even more nasty when you do by mistake something like
```julia
if condition1 && condition2 condition3 && condition4
```
`condition3 && condition4` aren't evaluated at all as part of the conditions of the `if`.  This should arguably be a parser/syntax error.  For example
```julia
julia> if true 1 2 3
           1
       end
ERROR: syntax: unexpected "2"
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```
`2` is a syntax error, I don't see why `1` shouldn't.

Other weird interpretations of this "feature":
```julia
julia> if true :foo
           1
       end
ERROR: UndefVarError: `foo` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[15]:1

julia> if true :2
           1
       end
ERROR: TypeError: non-boolean (UnitRange{Int64}) used in boolean context
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[17]:1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52014/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52014/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1792446929","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52014#issuecomment-1792446929","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52014","id":1792446929,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5q1pHR","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-11-03T13:34:48Z","updated_at":"2023-11-03T13:34:48Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Would it mean to also disallow one-liners in the form
```julia
if cond f() end
```
?

These one-liners are quite common, e.g.,:

This is faster.

Benchmarks using my new, in development benchmarking package. They only take 50s to reproduce (excluding time to install `Chairmarks`) and are pastable into a REPL.

```julia
using Random
import Pkg; Pkg.add(url="https://github.com/LilithHafner/Chairmarks.jl", rev="bbef8833c0a9ecb647181fbfbded0757ab52d094") # v0.1.0
using Chairmarks

function my_shuffle!(v::AbstractVector{Bool})
    old_sum = sum(v)
    x = 2old_sum <= length(v)
    fuel = x ? old_sum : length(v) - old_sum
    fuel == 0 && return v
    v .= !x
    while 0 < fuel
        k = rand(eachindex(v))
        fuel -= v[k] != x
        v[k] = x
    end
    v
end

@time for n in [0,1,2,3,10,30,100,300,1000,3000,10_000,100_000,10_000_000,100_000_000]
    for k in (n == 0 ? [0] : unique([0, 1, div(n,3), div(n,2), 2div(n, 3), n-1, n]))
        println("$k/$n")
        for T in [Vector, BitVector]
            print(lpad(T, 9), "  ")
            old = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            new = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) my_shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), old)
            print(" => ")
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), new)
            println(old.time < new.time ? " REGRESSION" : "")
        end
    end
end
```

This is faster.

Benchmarks using my new, in development benchmarking package. They only take 50s to reproduce (excluding time to install `Chairmarks`) and are pastable into a REPL.

```julia
using Random
import Pkg; Pkg.add(url="https://github.com/LilithHafner/Chairmarks.jl", rev="bbef8833c0a9ecb647181fbfbded0757ab52d094") # v0.1.0
using Chairmarks

function my_shuffle!(v::AbstractVector{Bool})
    old_sum = sum(v)
    x = 2old_sum <= length(v)
    fuel = x ? old_sum : length(v) - old_sum
    fuel == 0 && return v
    v .= !x
    while 0 < fuel
        k = rand(eachindex(v))
        fuel -= v[k] != x
        v[k] = x
    end
    v
end

@time for n in [0,1,2,3,10,30,100,300,1000,3000,10_000,100_000,10_000_000,100_000_000]
    for k in (n == 0 ? [0] : unique([0, 1, div(n,3), div(n,2), 2div(n, 3), n-1, n]))
        println("$k/$n")
        for T in [Vector, BitVector]
            print(lpad(T, 9), "  ")
            old = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            new = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) my_shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), old)
            print(" => ")
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), new)
            println(old.time < new.time ? " REGRESSION" : "")
        end
    end
end
```

This is faster.

Benchmarks using my new, in development benchmarking package. They only take 50s to reproduce (excluding time to install `Chairmarks`) and are pastable into a REPL.

```julia
using Random
import Pkg; Pkg.add(url="https://github.com/LilithHafner/Chairmarks.jl", rev="bbef8833c0a9ecb647181fbfbded0757ab52d094") # v0.1.0
using Chairmarks

function my_shuffle!(v::AbstractVector{Bool})
    old_sum = sum(v)
    x = 2old_sum <= length(v)
    fuel = x ? old_sum : length(v) - old_sum
    fuel == 0 && return v
    v .= !x
    while 0 < fuel
        k = rand(eachindex(v))
        fuel -= v[k] != x
        v[k] = x
    end
    v
end

@time for n in [0,1,2,3,10,30,100,300,1000,3000,10_000,100_000,10_000_000,100_000_000]
    for k in (n == 0 ? [0] : unique([0, 1, div(n,3), div(n,2), 2div(n, 3), n-1, n]))
        println("$k/$n")
        for T in [Vector, BitVector]
            print(lpad(T, 9), "  ")
            old = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            new = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) my_shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), old)
            print(" => ")
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), new)
            println(old.time < new.time ? " REGRESSION" : "")
        end
    end
end

This is faster.

Benchmarks using my new, in development benchmarking package. They only take 50s to reproduce (excluding time to install `Chairmarks`) and are pastable into a REPL.

```julia
using Random
import Pkg; Pkg.add(url="https://github.com/LilithHafner/Chairmarks.jl", rev="bbef8833c0a9ecb647181fbfbded0757ab52d094") # v0.1.0
using Chairmarks

function my_shuffle!(v::AbstractVector{Bool})
    old_sum = sum(v)
    x = 2old_sum <= length(v)
    fuel = x ? old_sum : length(v) - old_sum
    fuel == 0 && return v
    v .= !x
    while 0 < fuel
        k = rand(eachindex(v))
        fuel -= v[k] != x
        v[k] = x
    end
    v
end

@time for n in [0,1,2,3,10,30,100,300,1000,3000,10_000,100_000,10_000_000,100_000_000]
    for k in (n == 0 ? [0] : unique([0, 1, div(n,3), div(n,2), 2div(n, 3), n-1, n]))
        println("$k/$n")
        for T in [Vector, BitVector]
            print(lpad(T, 9), "  ")
            old = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            new = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) my_shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), old)
            print(" => ")
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), new)
            println(old.time < new.time ? " REGRESSION" : "")
        end
    end
end
```

This is faster.

Benchmarks using my new, in development benchmarking package. They only take 50s to reproduce (excluding time to install `Chairmarks`) and are pastable into a REPL.

```julia
using Random
import Pkg; Pkg.add(url="https://github.com/LilithHafner/Chairmarks.jl", rev="bbef8833c0a9ecb647181fbfbded0757ab52d094") # v0.1.0
using Chairmarks

function my_shuffle!(v::AbstractVector{Bool})
    old_sum = sum(v)
    x = 2old_sum <= length(v)
    fuel = x ? old_sum : length(v) - old_sum
    fuel == 0 && return v
    v .= !x
    while 0 < fuel
        k = rand(eachindex(v))
        fuel -= v[k] != x
        v[k] = x
    end
    v
end

@time for n in [0,1,2,3,10,30,100,300,1000,3000,10_000,100_000,10_000_000,100_000_000]
    for k in (n == 0 ? [0] : unique([0, 1, div(n,3), div(n,2), 2div(n, 3), n-1, n]))
        println("$k/$n")
        for T in [Vector, BitVector]
            print(lpad(T, 9), "  ")
            old = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            new = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) my_shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), old)
            print(" => ")
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), new)
            println(old.time < new.time ? " REGRESSION" : "")
        end
    end
end
```

This is faster.

Benchmarks using my new, in development benchmarking package. They only take 50s to reproduce (excluding time to install `Chairmarks`) and are pastable into a REPL.

```julia
using Random
import Pkg; Pkg.add(url="https://github.com/LilithHafner/Chairmarks.jl", rev="bbef8833c0a9ecb647181fbfbded0757ab52d094") # v0.1.0
using Chairmarks

function my_shuffle!(v::AbstractVector{Bool})
    old_sum = sum(v)
    x = 2old_sum <= length(v)
    fuel = x ? old_sum : length(v) - old_sum
    fuel == 0 && return v
    v .= !x
    while 0 < fuel
        k = rand(eachindex(v))
        fuel -= v[k] != x
        v[k] = x
    end
    v
end

@time for n in [0,1,2,3,10,30,100,300,1000,3000,10_000,100_000,10_000_000,100_000_000]
    for k in (n == 0 ? [0] : unique([0, 1, div(n,3), div(n,2), 2div(n, 3), n-1, n]))
        println("$k/$n")
        for T in [Vector, BitVector]
            print(lpad(T, 9), "  ")
            old = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            new = @b T(vcat(trues(k), falses(n-k))) my_shuffle! sum(_) == k || error()
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), old)
            print(" => ")
            show(stdout, MIME"text/plain"(), new)
            println(old.time < new.time ? " REGRESSION" : "")
        end
    end
end
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52330","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52330/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52330/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52330/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52330","id":2013925056,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM54Cg7A","number":52330,"title":"Markdown equation parsing error","user":{"login":"mauro3","id":4098145,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwOTgxNDU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4098145?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3","html_url":"https://github.com/mauro3","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mauro3/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-11-28T08:39:50Z","updated_at":"2023-11-28T09:26:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Consider:
```julia
               _
   _       _ _(_)_     |  Documentation: https://docs.julialang.org
  (_)     | (_) (_)    |
   _ _   _| |_  __ _   |  Type "?" for help, "]?" for Pkg help.
  | | | | | | |/ _` |  |
  | | |_| | | | (_| |  |  Version 1.9.4 (2023-11-14)
 _/ |\__'_|_|_|\__'_|  |  Official https://julialang.org/ release
|__/                   |

julia> using Markdown

julia> md"""
       \begin{equation}
       a_i
       \end{equation}
       $p_i$
       """
ERROR: UndefVarError: `p` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1
```

adding a space removes the errror:
```julia
julia> md"""
       \begin{equation}
       a_i
       \end{equation}
       $ p_i$
       """
  \begin{equation} ai \end{equation} $ pi$
```

Consider this:
```julia
julia> using Markdown

julia> md"$x"
ERROR: UndefVarError: `x` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> x = 1
1

julia> md"$x"
1
```

If you want to write out `$p_i$` (a TeX equation), you need to escape the `$` characters:
```julia
julia> md"\$p_i\$"
  $p_i$
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1829419651/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

It appears that in your example, the `equation` environment is not recognized as a math environment, and the underscore in `a_i` actually means to underline the following text.
```julia
julia> md"""
       \begin{equation}
       a_i
       \end{equation}
       x_
       """
  \begin{equation} a[i \end{equation} x] # brackets added to denote what is underlined in my terminal
```
Adding ` ```math ` helps:
```julia
julia> md"""
       ```math
       \begin{equation}
       a_i
       \end{equation}
       ```
       $x_i$
       """
  \begin{equation}
a_i
\end{equation}

  x_i
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1833387274/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-10-08T09:26:51Z","updated_at":"2023-10-09T07:08:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Having checked the documentation, I am inclined to support the view (expressed in the [Discourse discussion](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/julia-documentation-about-for-loop/104725)) that the `for` is not sufficiently documented. In particular, it is not even mentioned that anything else then a unit step (`for i = 1:3`) or enumeration (`for i in [1,4,0]`) is possible. It is not even mentioned that something like  (`for i = 1:2:5`) is possible. And no reference to the concept of an iterator (in this context) is given. 

The docstring only contains an example of an enumeration-based usage:
```
help?> for
search: for foreach foldr floor mapfoldr factorial EOFError OverflowError

  for


  for loops repeatedly evaluate a block of statements while iterating over a
  sequence of values.

  The iteration variable is always a new variable, even if a variable of the
  same name exists in the enclosing scope. Use outer to reuse an existing
  local variable for iteration.

  Examples
  ≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡

  julia> for i in [1, 4, 0]
             println(i)
         end
  1
  4
  0
```

Identical entry for the [for](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#for) keyword (what else would a beginner search for) the online manual.

Those who searched for something like *control flow* would find the section [Repeated evaluation: loops](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/control-flow/#man-loops). {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1374529913","pull_request_review_id":1701715359,"id":1374529913,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5R7al5","diff_hunk":"@@ -244,3 +244,30 @@ function withenv(f, keyvals::Pair{T}...) where T<:AbstractString
     end
 end
 withenv(f) = f() # handle empty keyvals case; see #10853
+
+"""
+    username() -> String
+
+Return the username for the current user. If the username cannot be found,
+this function throws an error.
+
+To retrieve a username that is overridable via an environment variable,
+e.g., `USER`, consider using
+```julia
+user = get(ENV, "USER", username())
+```
+
+!!! note
+    The username is determined via the user account and password database
+    that is available only on Unix-like platforms.
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.11"
+    This function requires at least Julia 1.11.
+"""
+function username()
+    uid = Libc.getuid()
+    username = Filesystem.getusername(uid)
+    isnothing(username) || return username
+    # the user was not found or the username was empty
+    throw(ErrorException("username not found in the password database"))","path":"base/env.jl","commit_id":"8e5319644e27992b2ddb8444aa9fc2a237606a20","original_commit_id":"8e5319644e27992b2ddb8444aa9fc2a237606a20","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Is But, when `some_name` is function, does that mean that *all* methods of `some_name` are meant for public usage?

If the answer is yes, IMO that could be problematic, but perhaps it makes sense at least as a default. I assume it could still be overridden by explicit documentation?

If the answer is no, it would be good to add an additional feature to be able to mark individual methods as `public`.

+
+To retrieve a username that is overridable via an environment variable,
+e.g., `USER`, consider using

+```

 withenv(f) = f() # handle empty keyvals case; see #10853
+
+"""
+    username() -> String
+
+Return the username for the current user. If the username cannot be found,
+this function throws an error.
+
+To retrieve a username that is overridable via an environment variable,
+e.g., `USER`, consider using
+```julia
+user = get(ENV, "USER", username())
+```
+
+!!! note
+    The username is determined via the user account and password database
+    that is available only on Unix-like platforms.","path":"base/env.jl","commit_id":"6219991c53dc43ea7f83b0eb63bd9254ee0b9ea3","original_commit_id":"8e5319644e27992b2ddb8444aa9fc2a237606a20","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Thank you, Steven. +@testset "username()" begin
+    if Sys.isunix()

+        @test username() == passwd.username
+    else

we add an optional API parameter for `sizehint!` that controls whether it is for `push!` (default) or `pushfirst!`. Secondly, we make the offset zero when using `sizehint!` to shrink an array from the end, or the trailing size zero when using it to shring from the beginning.

Then we replace the prior implementations of `prepend!` and `append!` with ones that are safe even if the iterator changes length during the operation or if convert fails. The result of `prepend!` may be in an undefined order (because of the `reverse!` call) in the presence of concurrent modifications or errors, but at least all of the elements will be present and valid afterwards.

Replaces and closes #49905
Replaces and closes #47391
Fixes #15868

Benchmarks show that repeated `push!` performance (with sizehint) is nearly equivalent to the old append performance:
```
julia> @benchmark append!(x, 1:1000) setup=x=Vector{Float64}(undef,0)

for first and make append!/prepend! safer","user":{"login":"vtjnash","id":330950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMzMDk1MA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/330950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash","html_url":"https://github.com/vtjnash","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vtjnash/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"First we add an optional API parameter for `sizehint!` that controls whether it is for `push!` (default) or `pushfirst!`. Secondly, we make the offset zero when using `sizehint!` to shrink an array from the end, or the trailing size zero when using it to shring from the beginning.

Then we replace the prior implementations of `prepend!` and `append!` with ones that are safe even if the iterator changes length during the operation or if convert fails. The result of `prepend!` may be in an undefined order (because of the `reverse!` call) in the presence of concurrent modifications or errors, but at least all of the elements will be present and valid afterwards.

Replaces and closes #49905
Replaces and closes #47391
Fixes #15868

Benchmarks show that repeated `push!` performance (with sizehint) is nearly equivalent to the old append performance:
```
julia> @benchmark append!(x, 1:1000) setup=x=Vector{Float64}(undef,0)

I put it to the `Libc` submodule because that's where the `Cpasswd` structure is defined.

> should maybe NOT export from Base,

I don't know. If `username` is supposed to be a part of the user-facing API, then I'd say it should be exported. If it is not supposed to be a part of the user-facing API, then there is little reason to add it at all because it is not used anywhere in Base.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51212","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51212/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51212/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51212/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51212","id":1882474132,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5wNEaU","number":51212,"title":"Avoid `count(itr)` confusion","user":{"login":"jariji","id":96840304,"node_id":"U_kgDOBcWqcA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96840304?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji","html_url":"https://github.com/jariji","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jariji/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-09-05T17:52:21Z","updated_at":"2023-09-06T09:27:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```jl
  count([f=identity,] itr; init=0) -> Integer
```


C++ has a lengthlike function `count` for getting the length of iterators and another function `count_if` that takes a predicate. 

In Julia, `count(itr)` looks like it counts the values in `itr`, but it actually only counts the `true` ones. Having the default `f` be `identity` rather than `Returns(true)` means `count(itr)` does the nonobvious thing. 

I propose deprecating `count(itr)` to avoid confusion.     mkpath(file)
end

You should definitely open a PR.

Based on the reviews in this PR, it seems that the function should always throw if the `uid` is not found, so you could remove the `throw_error` argument. Although the user-facing error should probably be more explanatory than what `Libc.getpwuid` gives:
```julia
julia> Libc.getpwuid(UInt(1232456))
ERROR: IOError: getpwuid: no such file or directory (ENOENT)
```
As Steven pointed out, it might be a good idea to throw an error even if the `username` field of the returned struct is empty.

The docs should probably also mention the hint that if you want to respect an environment variable, say `USER`, you should do `get(ENV, "USER", username())`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1725007609/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

PR resolves #47513.
Baseline: 1.10.0-alpha1

# Summary of the Implementation
1. ~~`cbrt(A::AbstractMatrix)` is delegated to `A^(1//3)`.~~ (Method removed.)
2. For real-valued `Diagonal` and `Symmetric` matrices, straightforward implementations based on scalar `cbrt` and `eigen` are provided.
3. For real-valued arbitrary matrices, a `schur` decomposition and [1, Algorithm 4.3]-based algorithm (`_cbrt_quasi_triu!`) is provided.
4. For real-valued upper and lower triangular matrices, straightforward solutions based on `_cbrt_quasi_triu!` are provided.

Note: See comment below for details on the algorithm.

## References
|  |   |
| ---- | ---- |
| [1]   | Smith, M. I. (2003). A Schur Algorithm for Computing Matrix pth Roots. In SIAM Journal on Matrix Analysis and Applications (Vol. 24, Issue 4, pp. 971–989). https://doi.org/10.1137/s0895479801392697 |

# Pending Items
- [X] ~~Memory and MIPS optimisations (trade-off) for `_cbrt_quasi_triu!`.~~

# Tests
File: `stdlib/LinearAlgebra/test/dense.jl`
Testset: `@testset "cbrt(A::AbstractMatrix{T})"` 

```julia
Test Summary:              | Pass  Total  Time
cbrt(A::AbstractMatrix{T}) |   24     24  0.0s
```

# Memory and MIPS
```julia
julia> A = randn(1000,1000) ;
julia> @btime cbrt(A) ;
  793.222 ms (246051 allocations: 47.96 MiB)
julia> @btime A^(1//3) ;
  5.448 s (154 allocations: 900.61 MiB)
julia> @btime sqrt(A) ;
  894.408 ms (24 allocations: 91.86 MiB)
```

- Target was to get lower than `sqrt` wrt MIPS and memory consumption, which is done now 🎉🎉🎉

PR resolves #47513.
Baseline: 1.10.0-alpha1

# Summary of the Implementation
1. ~~`cbrt(A::AbstractMatrix)` is delegated to `A^(1//3)`.~~ (Method removed.)
2. For real-valued `Diagonal` and `Symmetric` matrices, straightforward implementations based on scalar `cbrt` and `eigen` are provided.
3. For real-valued arbitrary matrices, a `schur` decomposition and [1, Algorithm 4.3]-based algorithm (`_cbrt_quasi_triu!`) is provided.
4. For real-valued upper and lower triangular matrices, straightforward solutions based on `_cbrt_quasi_triu!` are provided.

Note: See comment below for details on the algorithm.

## References
|  |   |
| ---- | ---- |
| [1]   | Smith, M. I. (2003). A Schur Algorithm for Computing Matrix pth Roots. In SIAM Journal on Matrix Analysis and Applications (Vol. 24, Issue 4, pp. 971–989). https://doi.org/10.1137/s0895479801392697 |

# Pending Items
- [X] ~~Memory and MIPS optimisations (trade-off) for `_cbrt_quasi_triu!`.~~

# Tests
File: `stdlib/LinearAlgebra/test/dense.jl`
Testset: `@testset "cbrt(A::AbstractMatrix{T})"` 

```julia
Test Summary:              | Pass  Total  Time
cbrt(A::AbstractMatrix{T}) |   24     24  0.0s
```

# Memory and MIPS
```julia
julia> A = randn(1000,1000) ;
julia> @btime cbrt(A) ;
  793.222 ms (246051 allocations: 47.96 MiB)
julia> @btime A^(1//3) ;
  5.448 s (154 allocations: 900.61 MiB)
julia> @btime sqrt(A) ;
  894.408 ms (24 allocations: 91.86 MiB)
```

- Target was to get lower than `sqrt` wrt MIPS and memory consumption, which is done now 🎉🎉🎉

Right now it only says "`DateTime` wraps a `UTInstant{Millisecond}` [...]".

Also, when constructing from a `Date` and a `Time`, `DateTime` does throw an `InexactError`:
```julia
julia> DateTime( Date(now()), Time(Nanosecond(1)) )
ERROR: InexactError: DateTime(00:00:00.000000001)
```
This behavior is [explicitly implemented](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/2c45e3ba9a2cf801d5b8da433552541c196ab54b/stdlib/Dates/src/types.jl#L395) as well as documented.

Maybe `toms` should throw an `InexactError` when its argument cannot be expressed as milliseconds (i.e., `Nanosecond` and `Microsecond` not divisible by 10^6 and 10^3, respectively)? Although, that would probably be breaking.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1665181455/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

#50785 

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

#50785 

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

noted that we have this:

```julia
help?> trunc
search: trunc truncate unsafe_trunc setrounding AbstractUnitRange set_zero_subnormals get_zero_subnormals

  trunc([T,] x)
⋮
  trunc(T, x) converts the result to type T, throwing an InexactError if the value is not representable.
⋮
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  trunc(dt::TimeType, ::Type{Period}) -> TimeType

  Truncates the value of dt according to the provided Period type.

  Examples
  ≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡≡

  julia> trunc(DateTime("1996-01-01T12:30:00"), Day)
  1996-01-01T00:00:00
```

`Dates` should not violate the normal argument order here, it should put the destination type first (just like `convert`, `round`, `floor`, etc..)

#50785 

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

The current version reflects my opinion.

When adding a `Period` to a `DateTime`, the `Period` is converted to `Millisecond`s, which are then added to the `DateTime`. The conversion issues a depwarn when it cannot be done losslessly now, but should throw an `InexactError` eventually (when `_convert` becomes `convert`). The user should decide if they want `x + trunc(Millisecond, y)` or `x + round(Millisecond, y)` or another alternative.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1682626960/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`DateTime`","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Resolves #50785 

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

cc @jariji #50785 

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

cc @jariji ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50816/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50816/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1683029999","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50816#issuecomment-1683029999","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50816","id":1683029999,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5kUP_v","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-17T21:55:13Z","updated_at":"2023-08-17T21:55:13Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I must stress that I *have not* encountered this problem in practice. I simply wanted to contribute, and since I find the following confusing,
```julia
x = DateTime(now())
y = Microsecond(1)
x + y == x # true
iszero(y) # false
```
I thought it would be best to disallow such behavior.

Having said that, I do not have a strong opinion about this, and I trust triage's judgment. Please, let me know how to proceed with this PR. Should I remove the second commit from this PR, or create a new PR?

----

Also, let me add that `DateTime` having millisecond resolution is not a mere technical detail. At least not now. Any function that invokes `toms` comes with a catch when called with a `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond` argument. Right now, `toms` is called by + and -, and also when constructing ranges. As a result, it is impossible to create a `DateTime` range with a step <1ms:
```julia
julia> length(DateTime("2023-08-17T00:00:00.00"):Millisecond(1):DateTime("2023-08-17T00:00:00.01"))
11

julia> length(DateTime("2023-08-17T00:00:00.00"):Microsecond(1):DateTime("2023-08-17T00:00:00.01"))
ERROR: InexactError: trunc(Int64, Inf)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1683029999/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

One interpretation is that `DateTime` represents a discrete quantity of some base unit (milliseconds now). Under this interpretation, I think, it is desirable to throw an `InexactError` when adding a period that is not representable as a whole number of the base unit. Since `DateTime` is basically documented as "the number of milliseconds", I've been inclined towards this interpretation.

The other interpretation is that `DateTime` is an approximation of a point on a continuous timeline. I don't think we should throw an `InexactError` in this case.

If the idea is to follow the second interpretation, we should do a better job documenting what the consequences are (both positive and negative) and how to live with them. I do not feel my first commit here is enough; in fact, I would even say that it should not be accepted either, since it promotes the first interpretation too.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1683980120/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#50785 

This is a proposal for fixing the linked issue. The PR consists of two commits. 

The first commit just adds a few tests and one sentence to the docs of `DateTime`. I think this could be merged.

The second commit changes addition/subtraction of a `DateTime` and `Microsecond`/`Nanosecond`. 
Before:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # truncates
2023-08-07T00:00:00

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
After:
```julia
julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 microsecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(1) # throws
ERROR: InexactError: +(Millisecond, 1 nanosecond)

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Microsecond(10^3)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001

julia> DateTime(2023, 08, 07) + Nanosecond(10^6)
2023-08-07T00:00:00.001
```
The second commit is breaking so I am not sure if it can be merged.

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50206

Moves the pidfile from the package source to the depot. The pidlock is now specific to the cachefile but ignoring any preferences (that may change the cachefile path) as the prefs hash can only be determined after precompilation.

@vchuravy 

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50213

Stale pidfile rejection was unintentionally disabled, `stale_age` should have been set. I'm proposing 60 seconds. See comments for explanation.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345","id":1780408754,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5qHuGy","number":50345,"title":"Memory leak in Base","user":{"login":"schlichtanders","id":4255480,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyNTU0ODA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4255480?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders","html_url":"https://github.com/schlichtanders","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-06-29T08:56:26Z","updated_at":"2023-06-29T11:43:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi there

I found a small example to reproduce a memory leak which kept me busy for several days. (Hoping to find a workaround soon).

Running the following in Julia REPL several times will show an increase in memory usage (about 200 KB per run). 
```julia
begin
forecast_samples = [randn(30) for i in 1:10_000]
sums = vec(mapslices(sum, reduce(vcat, forecast_samples'), dims=1))
GC.gc(); Base.gc_live_bytes() / 2^20
end
```

For sure this example is not yet minimal, but at least super tiny.

(tested on Julia 1.9.0 and 1.9.1)


EDIT: Just realized that gc_live_bytes is quite noisy. Hence better do the above a couple of times. E.g.
```julia
[begin
forecast_samples = [randn(30) for i in 1:10_000]
sums = vec(mapslices(sum, reduce(vcat, forecast_samples'), dims=1))
GC.gc(); Base.gc_live_bytes() / 2^20
end for i in 1:50]
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":2,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1612982130","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345#issuecomment-1612982130","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50345","id":1612982130,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5gJCdy","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-06-29T11:43:01Z","updated_at":"2023-06-29T11:43:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I think the example can be reduced to a `reduce` of `vcat` over an adjoint.
```julia
const forecast_samples     = [randn(30) for i in 1:10_000];
const forecast_samples_adj = [randn(30) for i in 1:10_000]';

function f(arr)
    reduce(vcat, arr)
    GC.gc(true)
    GC.gc(false)
    return Base.gc_live_bytes() / 2^20
end
```

2. I Installed Julia using an installer downloaded from julialang.org
3. MWE
```
r = LinRange(Float16(0.0), Float16(20.0*pi), 1024)
h = Float16(0.01)

id_no_collect = findfirst((r .+ h) .== r)        # 253
id_collect = findfirst((collect(r) .+ h) .== r)  # 522
```

As indicated above, the result of adding a scalar to a `LinRange` depends on whether `collect` is applied to it first. Is this desired/expected behaviour?
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49621/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49621/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1534287519","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49621#issuecomment-1534287519","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49621","id":1534287519,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5bc16f","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-04T08:27:13Z","updated_at":"2023-05-04T08:27:13Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I am not sure if the behavior is desired but [it is known](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45947#issuecomment-1422309467).

The result of `r .+ h` is a range again:
```julia
julia> typeof(r .+ h)
LinRange{Float16, Int64}
```
and I believe that the computation is done as follows:
```julia
result = LinRange(first(r) + h, last(r) + h, 1024);
# result == (r .+ h) is true
```
which is theoretically correct but not equivalent to `collect(r) .+ h` due to numerical inaccuracies.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1534287519/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Base64","user":{"login":"t-f-marfil","id":74968391,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc0OTY4Mzkx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/74968391?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil","html_url":"https://github.com/t-f-marfil","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/t-f-marfil/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-05-16T22:20:10Z","updated_at":"2023-05-17T11:23:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49849","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49849","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49849.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49849.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR introduces base64url and base64/base64url encoding with no padding to Base64 module.

Implementing base64url in stdlib has been discussed in Julia Discourse,
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/support-for-urlsafe-alphabets-in-base64/4065
and more recently, in #49060.
A PR that implements base64url, #29318, was once made, but was closed before being merged (this was done before Issue #49060 was made, and I am afraid I do not know well the background and reasons why the PR was not merged). Therefore, I still think it is good to have base64url in stdlib/Base64.

This PR adds base64url and an option to enable/disable padding, which can be used along with base64url, as mentioned in RFC4648.

Implementation style is not so different from that of #29318, and thus I would similarly show the result of some benchmarks to show that severe performance regressions were not found so far.

In the code block below original Base64 module is referred to as `Base64` and the one with this PR as `Base64url`.

```julia
julia> @benchmark Base64.base64decode(dectest)

BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 28 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  878.571 ns … 82.350 μs  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 98.06%
 Time  (median):     975.000 ns              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):     1.257 μs ±  2.541 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  7.05% ±  3.65%

 Memory estimate: 2.30 KiB, allocs estimate: 31.

julia> @benchmark Base64url.base64decode(dectest)

BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 38 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  902.632 ns … 75.274 μs  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 97.06%
 Time  (median):     973.684 ns              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):     1.310 μs ±  2.539 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  8.17% ±  4.24%

 Memory estimate: 2.30 KiB, allocs estimate: 31.

julia> @benchmark Base64.base64encode(enctest)

BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 178 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  584.831 ns … 636.268 μs  ┊ GC (min … max):  0.00% … 96.53%
 Time  (median):     616.854 ns               ┊ GC (median):     0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):     1.430 μs ±  12.126 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  42.82% ±  5.80%

 Memory estimate: 1.11 KiB, allocs estimate: 10.

julia> @benchmark Base64url.base64encode(enctest)

BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 183 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  569.399 ns … 709.499 μs  ┊ GC (min … max):  0.00% … 97.79%
 Time  (median):     618.579 ns               ┊ GC (median):     0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):     1.445 μs ±  12.803 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  44.52% ±  5.87%

 Memory estimate: 1.11 KiB, allocs estimate: 10.

julia> @benchmark Base64url.base64encode(enctest, padding=false)

BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 182 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  561.538 ns … 690.309 μs  ┊ GC (min … max):  0.00% … 97.50%
 Time  (median):     601.099 ns               ┊ GC (median):     0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):     1.429 μs ±  12.433 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  44.12% ±  5.87%

 Memory estimate: 1.11 KiB, allocs estimate: 10.
```

This PR also includes minor fix in function argument type (`(f::Function, ...)` to `(f::F, ...) where {F<:Function}`), which slightly improves encoding performance from above results.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49849/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49849/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1551212155","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49849#issuecomment-1551212155","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49849","id":1551212155,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5cdZ57","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-17T11:23:41Z","updated_at":"2023-05-17T11:23:41Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I am reading the Discourse thread and the API discussed there is more general. >The main use of an LDLt factorization F = ldlt(S) is [...]. This is the return type of ldlt, the corresponding matrix factorization function.

Nevertheless, some methods for `LDLt` seem to assume that its `data` matrix is of type `SymTridiagonal`. Shouldn't we restrict the type then?
```julia
struct LDLt{T,S<:AbstractMatrix{T}}  # current
struct LDLt{T,S<:SymTridiagonal{T}}  # proposed
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1497069863/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880","id":1608217423,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5f23NP","number":48880,"title":"Bad performance getting R factor from QR","user":{"login":"fmariofilho","id":84345325,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg0MzQ1MzI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/84345325?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho","html_url":"https://github.com/fmariofilho","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fmariofilho/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-03-03T08:59:47Z","updated_at":"2023-03-03T11:44:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Since the library uses `getproperty` to make and return the `R` factor of a QR factorization (when you write `fac.R`), it keeps recomputing the factor, which leads for very bad performance.  For instance, the following code is very slow:

```
A = # some matrix
fac = qr(A)
diag = [ fac.R[i, i] for i = 1:size(fac.R, 1) ]
```
It would be better to cache the result or, instead of using getproperty, make a function to return the `R` factor.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1453409932","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880#issuecomment-1453409932","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48880","id":1453409932,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5WoUaM","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-03T11:44:54Z","updated_at":"2023-03-03T11:44:54Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The factors are not recomputed to be precise. They are extracted to newly allocated matrices when the property is requested.

Do you think it would be sufficient to advice the user to cache the factors in the documentation, instead of introducing a new functions for that? Note that the following is already possible so maybe it should also be more advertised:
```julia
julia> A = [3.0 -6.0; 4.0 -8.0; 0.0 1.0]
3×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 3.0  -6.0
 4.0  -8.0
 0.0   1.0

julia> Q, R = qr(A)
LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWY{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}
Q factor:
3×3 LinearAlgebra.QRCompactWYQ{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}:
 -0.6   0.0   0.8
 -0.8   0.0  -0.6
  0.0  -1.0   0.0
R factor:
2×2 Matrix{Float64}:
 -5.0  10.0
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1453409932/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+@testset "username" begin

+        local uname = "testuser"

+        @test withenv(varname => uname, unset_envdict...) do
+            username()
+        end == uname
+    end
+

believe `for f in (rad2deg, deg2rad)` should iterate over symbols: `for f in (:rad2deg, :deg2rad)`. Although I am not sure if that is what caused the build failure.

However, I still have doubts about the numerical accuracy. For example:
```julia
julia> collect(rad2deg(2:10)) == rad2deg.(2:10)
true
# but
julia> collect(rad2deg(1:10)) == rad2deg.(1:10)
false
```

The broadcasting approach simply applies function `f` to every element of range `r`, resulting in an array `a`, hence `a[i] = f(r[i])`. On the contrary, as I understand it, your approach boils down to `a[i] = f(first(r)) + (i - 1) * s`, where `s = f(step(r))` or `s = (f(last(r)) - f(first(r))) / length(r)` depending on the type of `r`. So despite being theoretically correct for a linear `f`, it seems to me that it will always be less numerically accurate than the broadcasting approach. I might be wrong though.

(Have you considered [JuliaArrays/MappedArrays.jl](https://github.com/JuliaArrays/MappedArrays.jl) for your use-case? It supports ranges as well.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1422285152/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`allunique`","user":{"login":"mcabbott","id":32575566,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNTc1NTY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/32575566?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott","html_url":"https://github.com/mcabbott","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mcabbott/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Closes #47005, and does the same for `allunique`.

First, a33ac25e9e5375b71464d7003edcd5bb14b1da85 uses `Iterators.peel` to improve `allequal(itr)` as suggested by @jlapeyre in here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005#issuecomment-1276477572 . 

After that, `allequal(f, xs) = allequal(f(x) for x in xs)` should never call `f` twice on the same `x`, nor more times than necessary. 

For `allunique`, at present this has a path which collects short iterators (<32) as this is faster than making a `Set`. Thus calling `allunique(f(x) for x in xs)` will sometimes call `f` more times than it has to. This PR doesn't change that, just adds a method `allunique(f, xs)`.

Since generators tend to have eltype `Any`, it pays to use `Set{@default_eltype(C)}()` now. I think this ought to be safe.

Finally, methods `allequal(f, xs::Tuple)` and `allunique(f, xs::Tuple)` seem to be worthwhile. [This gist](https://gist.github.com/mcabbott/69c0b85cdf0a37d97eecba3edb18bdc0) times a few #47005, and does the same for `allunique`.

First, a33ac25e9e5375b71464d7003edcd5bb14b1da85 uses `Iterators.peel` to improve `allequal(itr)` as suggested by @jlapeyre in here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005#issuecomment-1276477572 . 

After that, `allequal(f, xs) = allequal(f(x) for x in xs)` should never call `f` twice on the same `x`, nor more times than necessary. 

For `allunique`, at present this has a path which collects short iterators (<32) as this is faster than making a `Set`. Thus calling `allunique(f(x) for x in xs)` will sometimes call `f` more times than it has to. This PR doesn't change that, just adds a method `allunique(f, xs)`.

Since generators tend to have eltype `Any`, it pays to use `Set{@default_eltype(C)}()` now. I think this ought to be safe.

Finally, methods `allequal(f, xs::Tuple)` and `allunique(f, xs::Tuple)` seem to be worthwhile. [This gist](https://gist.github.com/mcabbott/69c0b85cdf0a37d97eecba3edb18bdc0) times a few #47005, and does the same for `allunique`.

First, a33ac25e9e5375b71464d7003edcd5bb14b1da85 uses `Iterators.peel` to improve `allequal(itr)` as suggested by @jlapeyre in here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005#issuecomment-1276477572 . 

After that, `allequal(f, xs) = allequal(f(x) for x in xs)` should never call `f` twice on the same `x`, nor more times than necessary. 

For `allunique`, at present this has a path which collects short iterators (<32) as this is faster than making a `Set`. Thus calling `allunique(f(x) for x in xs)` will sometimes call `f` more times than it has to. This PR doesn't change that, just adds a method `allunique(f, xs)`.

Since generators tend to have eltype `Any`, it pays to use `Set{@default_eltype(C)}()` now. I think this ought to be safe.

Finally, methods `allequal(f, xs::Tuple)` and `allunique(f, xs::Tuple)` seem to be worthwhile. [This gist](https://gist.github.com/mcabbott/69c0b85cdf0a37d97eecba3edb18bdc0) times a few #47005, and does the same for `allunique`.

First, a33ac25e9e5375b71464d7003edcd5bb14b1da85 uses `Iterators.peel` to improve `allequal(itr)` as suggested by @jlapeyre in here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47005#issuecomment-1276477572 . 

After that, `allequal(f, xs) = allequal(f(x) for x in xs)` should never call `f` twice on the same `x`, nor more times than necessary. 

For `allunique`, at present this has a path which collects short iterators (<32) as this is faster than making a `Set`. Thus calling `allunique(f(x) for x in xs)` will sometimes call `f` more times than it has to. This PR doesn't change that, just adds a method `allunique(f, xs)`.

Since generators tend to have eltype `Any`, it pays to use `Set{@default_eltype(C)}()` now. I think this ought to be safe.

Finally, methods `allequal(f, xs::Tuple)` and `allunique(f, xs::Tuple)` seem to be worthwhile. [This gist](https://gist.github.com/mcabbott/69c0b85cdf0a37d97eecba3edb18bdc0) times a few module"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-01-23T10:56:15Z","updated_at":"2023-01-23T14:31:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I have noticed that the comparison functions of `Dates.CompoundPeriod` don't produce the expected output with certain values .

Here is an example:
```Julia
using Dates
a = Week(33293) + Day(3) + Hour(5) + Minute(15) + Second(44)
b = Week(9041) + Day(4) + Hour(5) + Minute(58) + Second(24)

println(a < b)
```
With Julia version 1.8.3 and 1.8.5 it produces
```Bash
true
```

I would have expected that to be false (or I misunderstand this comparison?).

So what if we changed it to something like this:
```julia
function Base.isless(x::CompoundPeriod, y::CompoundPeriod)
    diff_ns = 0.0
    nperiods = min(length(x.periods), length(y.periods))
    for (xp, yp) in zip(x.periods[:nperiods], y.periods[:nperiods])
        diff_ns += (tons(xp) - tons(yp))
    end
    diff_ns += sum(tons, x.periods[(nperiods + 1):])
    diff_ns -= sum(tons, y.periods[(nperiods + 1):])
    return diff_ns < 0
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1400444019/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452","id":1562048892,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5dGvl8","number":48452,"title":"Possibly unintended behavior of `zero(Time)`","user":{"login":"vitornesello","id":12225826,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMjI1ODI2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12225826?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello","html_url":"https://github.com/vitornesello","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vitornesello/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-01-30T09:22:17Z","updated_at":"2023-01-30T09:54:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I would expect `zero(Time)` to return an object of type `Time`. Instead it returns a `Nanosecond`. See the minimal working example below, where I compare it with `typemin`:

```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.8.5
Commit 17cfb8e65ea (2023-01-08 06:45 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (x86_64-apple-darwin21.4.0)
  CPU: 10 × Apple M1 Pro
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-13.0.1 (ORCJIT, westmere)
  Threads: 1 on 10 virtual cores
Environment:
  LD_LIBRARY_PATH = :/Library/gurobi910/mac64//lib:/Library/gurobi910/mac64//lib

julia> using Dates

julia> zero(Time)
0 nanoseconds

julia> typemin(Time)
00:00:00

julia> typemin(Time) == zero(Time)
false

julia> typemin(Time) === zero(Time)
false

julia> typeof(zero(Time))
Nanosecond

julia> typeof(typemin(Time))
Time

julia> Nanosecond <: Time
false
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1408304177","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452#issuecomment-1408304177","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48452","id":1408304177,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5T8QQx","user":{"login":"barucden","id":13768925,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzY4OTI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13768925?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden","html_url":"https://github.com/barucden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/barucden/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-01-30T09:54:21Z","updated_at":"2023-01-30T09:54:21Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"`zero(x)` returns the additive identity for `x`. So `x + zero(x) == x` should hold, and it does for a `Time`:
```julia
julia> t = Time("12:34pm", "HH:MMp")
12:34:00

julia> t + zero(t) == t
true
```

The [definition](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/stdlib/Dates/src/types.jl#L437-L441) of `Base.zero(::Type{Time})` contains the comment "zero returns dt::T - dt::T". The difference of `t - t`, where `t` is a `Time`, really returns a `Nanosecond`:
```julia
julia> t - t
0 nanoseconds

julia> typeof(t - t)
Nanosecond
```
`Time` "represents a specific moment in a 24-hour day", whereas `Nanosecond` is a time period. Adding a time period to a time results in a time again. Overall, it seems that this is intentional.
